textDisplay: TULSA DAILY WORLD; Get into Arresting Habit. Osage County Officers Are Still Looking for Brown Murderer; Tuesday, August 30, 1921; FAIRFAX, August 29--Bryant and Ernest Burkhart, taxi owners and drivers of Fairfax, have been arrested and confined in the Osage County Jail for alleged complicity in the murder of Mrs. Anna Brown, the Osage Indian wife of Oat Brow of Ponca City, about 90 days ago near Grayhorse in the western Osage. The woman was found in the brush with a bullet hole in the top of her head. Thus far, six different persons have been arrested but four have been released after examination. Officers are working on the theory that the murder was committed to cover up robbery. In probing this case, a continuous coroner's jury is held, meeting whenever desired at the call of the presiding official to hear new evidence.

textDisplay: If you have the chance, go see it in the movie theatre, before it is taken away. I think its great cinematography  requires for it to be seen on a big screen in order to be appreciated at its fullest. Actually, I think everything in this movie is to be seen and enjoyed on the big screen, the settings, the acting, the sound and the music, but, above all, it is a very interesting and well told story based on facts (most tragic and horrific ones ). Scorsese is at the peak of his powers as a movie maker, and all the cast ensemble is simply terrific.

textDisplay:
Another Native American story that should be told, but that has not really been ever told on a national level: Henry Berry Lowry.

Henry Berry Lowry was basically a real life Robin Hood crossed with Django unchained. Lumbee were being actively enslaved by the confederacy to build fortifications on the coast and having their land seized during the civil war. So it was deeply personal when Henry Berry Lowry launched a guerilla style war, inglorious basterds style against confederates and plantation owners across southern NC. With a multi racial gang/group of supporters, he would show up to Confederates churches, plantations, etc. and burn, loot and free slaves. The man killed Confederate home guard soldiers and would show up silent like the wind in the homes of the families of those he killed and apologize for the life he had taken. Lowry once took out an entire platoon of Confederate home guard with only a canoe and a pistol. Loot he stole, he redistributed to the poor of Robeson county - whether they were white, black or native.

Man was so prolific that he was the most wanted man in America for decades in an era that people otherwise remember Wild West outlaws. This and Lumbee history of opposing the racism in the South, from serving as scouts to Sherman's March or actively terrorizing the Klan out of their territory made it so despite being one of the largest Native American peoples in the US and the largest on the east coast, they never received full Federal Recognition
textOriginal:
Another Native American story that should be told, but that has not really been ever told on a national level: Henry Berry Lowry.

Henry Berry Lowry was basically a real life Robin Hood crossed with Django unchained. Lumbee were being actively enslaved by the confederacy to build fortifications on the coast and having their land seized during the civil war. So it was deeply personal when Henry Berry Lowry launched a guerilla style war, inglorious basterds style against confederates and plantation owners across southern NC. With a multi racial gang/group of supporters, he would show up to Confederates churches, plantations, etc. and burn, loot and free slaves. The man killed Confederate home guard soldiers and would show up silent like the wind in the homes of the families of those he killed and apologize for the life he had taken. Lowry once took out an entire platoon of Confederate home guard with only a canoe and a pistol. Loot he stole, he redistributed to the poor of Robeson county - whether they were white, black or native.

Man was so prolific that he was the most wanted man in America for decades in an era that people otherwise remember Wild West outlaws. This and Lumbee history of opposing the racism in the South, from serving as scouts to Sherman's March or actively terrorizing the Klan out of their territory made it so despite being one of the largest Native American peoples in the US and the largest on the east coast, they never received full Federal Recognition

textDisplay: This was truly a great film! It provided a  historically accurate detailing of events by the Osage Tribe.  Although we don't always want to pull up the past because it isn’t pretty, the point  ow is to remember and highlight what happened to this tribe in Oklahoma.   The people that were involved in these  events are the ones responsible for the decisions that they made and for what transpired afterwards. As the viewer, we can appreciate it for what it is through Scorsese a glimpse of native american history.  DiCaprio’s and Gladstone’s acting had me believing that he could be the marrying kind. The wide range of characters in this film can’t be praised enough. Enjoyed this movie very much!
textOriginal: This was truly a great film! It provided a  historically accurate detailing of events by the Osage Tribe.  Although we don't always want to pull up the past because it isn’t pretty, the point  ow is to remember and highlight what happened to this tribe in Oklahoma.   The people that were involved in these  events are the ones responsible for the decisions that they made and for what transpired afterwards. As the viewer, we can appreciate it for what it is through Scorsese a glimpse of native american history.  DiCaprio’s and Gladstone’s acting had me believing that he could be the marrying kind. The wide range of characters in this film can’t be praised enough. Enjoyed this movie very much!

textDisplay: 'Killers of the Flower Moon' was incredible. It is a cinematic masterpiece, featuring transformative performances from Leonardo DiCaprio and Robert De Niro. The entire cast delivers outstanding acting, making this true story adaptation Oscar-worthy. The film's historical accuracy and attention to detail immerse the audience in a gripping narrative. It sheds light on a dark chapter in American history, offering a thought-provoking exploration of greed and power. Martin Scorsese's direction elevates this film to a must-see cinematic experience.
textOriginal: 'Killers of the Flower Moon' was incredible. It is a cinematic masterpiece, featuring transformative performances from Leonardo DiCaprio and Robert De Niro. The entire cast delivers outstanding acting, making this true story adaptation Oscar-worthy. The film's historical accuracy and attention to detail immerse the audience in a gripping narrative. It sheds light on a dark chapter in American history, offering a thought-provoking exploration of greed and power. Martin Scorsese's direction elevates this film to a must-see cinematic experience.

Robbie Robertson (rip),sounds like an incredible score - the use of voice sounds incredibly primal and harrowing. 

Buzzards circling, what they think is theirs as a birth rite. 

The use of wide and master shots trying to capture the scope of this land and people (Osage) is quite poignant, how did we not know of this event in History sooner (Clearly narrations where written and presented of the 'Osage reign of terror', but representatives of that community were all disregarded - important to question authorship of historians, where they looked for their documentation)? Quite the revelation, do read the book!

textDisplay: For a movie this long you need to embed yourself in these characters and you will see that time isn't an issue.  True facts of how the Osage Nation bought land in Oklahoma and not long after, they discovered oil and they became the richest tribe in the world. To the schemers who wanted to get their grips on ownership of the oil, they insidiously start to marry in to the Osage women. It's absolutely disgusting, sickening and terrifying how they took over. This movie will, or certainly needs to be up for Oscars on all levels.

textDisplay:
Lily Gladstone, born in Kalispell, Montana, is a remarkable actress whose performances have captured the hearts of audiences and critics alike. Her journey from the picturesque landscapes of Montana to the bustling streets of Hollywood has made her a rising star known for her grace and versatility on screen.
Raised on the Blackfeet Reservation in Montana, Lily has always had a deep connection to her Tribal heritage. Her upbringing instilled in her a profound appreciation for storytelling and the natural world, which would later become essential elements of her artistic expression.
Gladstone's breakout moment came with her role in "Certain Women" (2016), a film directed by Kelly Reichardt. In this anthology film, she portrayed Jamie, a quiet ranch hand who becomes infatuated with an out-of-town lawyer, beautifully capturing the longing and isolation of her character. Her performance was great, earning her critical acclaim and paving the way for further opportunities in the industry.
For all you gamers out there, she also played a main role in the critically acclaimed video game "The Last of Us Part II" (2020), where she lent her voice and likeness to the character of "Dina." The game's emotionally charged complex characters resonated with players worldwide, and Gladstone's contribution was integral to its success.
Her most recent as well as biggest project, "Killers of the Flower Moon," is a testament to her commitment of telling Native stories. Directed by Martin Scorsese, and starring Leonardo DiCaprio the film explores the tragedy of the Osage people of Oklahoma. Gladstone's portrayal of Mollie Burkhart, should deliver a performance nothing short of remarkable.
Beyond her acting prowess, she is an advocate for Native representation in Hollywood. She believes in the power of storytelling to foster understanding and empathy across cultures and strives to be a voice for Tribal communities.
In a world where diversity and inclusion are paramount, her legacy underscores the importance of authentic representation while breaking down barriers in the entertainment industry.
Although Buffy Sainte-Marie, was the first Native woman to win an Oscar for best original music, congratulations by the way. Here’s to hoping Lily can be the first Native Woman to win for acting. I have a very strong feeling that she will.

If you enjoyed this bio go to 
www.iknowanative.com

textDisplay: As a person of Osage descent living near where this was filmed, I am genuinely looking forward to a film for the first time in a long time.  From what I can tell they have recreated this story to a tee while being historically accurate which is commendable.  They went to the extent of doing renovations to the entire downtown Pawhuska to make it as close to what it looked like in 1920 as possible.
textOriginal: As a person of Osage descent living near where this was filmed, I am genuinely looking forward to a film for the first time in a long time.  From what I can tell they have recreated this story to a tee while being historically accurate which is commendable.  They went to the extent of doing renovations to the entire downtown Pawhuska to make it as close to what it looked like in 1920 as possible.

textOriginal: @ironwolf1799 as far as I know, yes they did. textDisplay:
Martin Scorsese's directorial prowess is on full display, and the chemistry between Leonardo DiCaprio and Robert De Niro is palpable. This trailer offers just enough of a tantalizing glimpse into the story's intrigue without giving away too much, leaving me eager to uncover the secrets of the Osage murders.

The historical backdrop and the exploration of this dark chapter in American history add layers of depth to the narrative. I'm already captivated by the suspense and the promise of a gripping tale of crime, conspiracy, and justice.

"Killers of the Flower Moon" looks like a cinematic gem in the making. With a stellar cast and Scorsese's visionary direction, this film is undoubtedly one of the most anticipated releases of the year for film enthusiasts like me. I can't wait to see how this captivating story unfolds on the big screen.

textDisplay:
A three and a half hour blockbuster epic by the most acclaimed living director starring the two highest-profile critically acclaimed living lead actors, based on a best-selling massively acclaimed book, itself not even the first acclaimed book on the topic (the one before it, "Mean Spirit", won the highest literary journalism prize possible), is hardly "brushing over".

What exactly in history courses in American schools is highlighting anything OTHER than "America's dark history"? If the Osage murders are not covered, it's only because they've been crowded out by covering slavery, or segregation, or Tuskegee medical experimentation, or Emmet Till, etc etc etc.

The only reasonable criticism of American history courses is that they don't cover anything OTHER than "America's dark history". Growing up in American schools you would imagine America is somehow uniquely evil and that there is nothing else significant in our history than nonstop racism.
textOriginal:
A three and a half hour blockbuster epic by the most acclaimed living director starring the two highest-profile critically acclaimed living lead actors, based on a best-selling massively acclaimed book, itself not even the first acclaimed book on the topic (the one before it, "Mean Spirit", won the highest literary journalism prize possible), is hardly "brushing over".

What exactly in history courses in American schools is highlighting anything OTHER than "America's dark history"? If the Osage murders are not covered, it's only because they've been crowded out by covering slavery, or segregation, or Tuskegee medical experimentation, or Emmet Till, etc etc etc.

The only reasonable criticism of American history courses is that they don't cover anything OTHER than "America's dark history". Growing up in American schools you would imagine America is somehow uniquely evil and that there is nothing else significant in our history than nonstop racism.

textDisplay: ​@LiftHeavy26where do you think addiction comes from? Just curious if you've ever considered the neurobiological cause for individuals who feel the need to self treat with alcohol or drugs? It's a poor coping mechanism, but an easy one to fall into. Just because it's at one's own hand doesn't remove the genetic or societal component to their suffering. You might want to learn to practice a little empathy towards your ancestry. Great that you're not afflicted, but maybe you are. Sounds like you have some sour grapes about your heritage. Is it possible that psychologically your critique is so harsh because of either a fear of being the same or as part of your coping mechanisms ? Just curious.

textDisplay: Looks miles better than Scorsese’s last big picture "The Irishman". Better concept, the premise is more interesting, and an era in western history that doesn't really get a lot of attention for motion picture storytelling, like the American Civil Rights movement which is just an absolute snooze that's been run into the ground. There are more historical eras, figures, and events to make movies about.

textDisplay: If you're looking for movies with Native American representation then be sure to check out "The Missing" from 2003, if you haven't seen it already.  When it first arrived in theaters Native Americans were literally organizing groups to bus to theaters miles away to see it, because authentic Chiricauhua Apache was spoken throughout the movie, something you never see.
textOriginal: If you're looking for movies with Native American representation then be sure to check out "The Missing" from 2003, if you haven't seen it already.  When it first arrived in theaters Native Americans were literally organizing groups to bus to theaters miles away to see it, because authentic Chiricauhua Apache was spoken throughout the movie, something you never see.
